questions.question: What is an exclusion or exemption clause?

options.A: A clause that defines the obligations the parties have agreed to perform
options.B: A clause that attempts to exclude or limit one party's liability for breach of contract or negligence
options.C: A clause that excuses performance where it becomes commercially inconvenient
options.D: A clause that gives one party the right to vary the terms of the contract

questions.explanation: An exclusion (or exemption) clause is a contractual term by which one party seeks to exclude or limit their liability for breach of contract, negligence, or other wrongful acts. Courts have developed strict rules of incorporation and construction to control their use, and statute (particularly consumer legislation) restricts or prohibits their use in certain contexts.

questions.question: For an exclusion clause to be binding, it must first be incorporated into the contract. Which of the following is a recognised method of incorporation?

options.A: Displaying the clause on a notice board at the seller's premises after the contract is made
options.B: Signature, notice given before or at the time of contracting, or by a course of dealing between the parties
options.C: Sending the clause to the other party after the contract has been signed
options.D: Including the clause only in the seller's internal documentation

questions.explanation: An exclusion clause may be incorporated by: (1) signature — a party who signs a contractual document is bound by its terms regardless of whether they read them (L'Estrange v Graucob [1934]); (2) notice — the clause must be brought to the other party's attention before or at the time of contracting (Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971]); or (3) a consistent course of prior dealings (Spurling v Bradshaw [1956]).

questions.question: In L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd [1934], the Court of Appeal held that a woman who signed a contract without reading it was bound by the exclusion clause it contained. This establishes which principle?

options.A: Exclusion clauses must be communicated in plain language to be binding
options.B: A party who signs a contractual document is bound by all its terms, including exclusion clauses, whether or not they were read
options.C: Exclusion clauses in standard form contracts are always voidable
options.D: Illiteracy or inability to read is a defence to contractual exclusion clauses

questions.explanation: In L'Estrange v Graucob [1934], the Court of Appeal held that a person who signs a written contract is bound by all its terms — including an exclusion clause — even if they have not read the document. The rule is subject to exceptions for misrepresentation about the nature or contents of the document (as in Curtis v Chemical Cleaning [1951]) and the doctrine of non est factum.

questions.question: In Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971], Lord Denning held that an exclusion clause on a ticket issued by a machine was not incorporated because:

options.A: The clause was printed in sufficiently large font to be readable
options.B: The contract was formed when the customer drove up to the machine and the ticket was issued — the clause on the ticket came after contract formation
options.C: The clause was not signed by both parties
options.D: Exclusion clauses for personal injury are always invalid in parking contracts

questions.explanation: In Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971], Lord Denning held that the contract was concluded when the customer drove up to the barrier, which automatically raised. The ticket was issued afterwards — it was too late to incorporate the exclusion clause printed on it. The offer and acceptance had already been completed before the clause was communicated.

questions.question: What is the 'red hand rule' associated with Spurling v Bradshaw [1956] and Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971]?

options.A: A rule that exclusion clauses are automatically void if not drawn to the other party's attention by the use of red ink
options.B: The principle that the more onerous or unusual the exclusion clause, the greater the degree of notice required to incorporate it — a particularly unusual clause may need to be pointed out in red ink with a pointing hand
options.C: A rule that exclusion clauses relating to personal injury must be in red to be valid
options.D: A rule that all exclusion clauses in consumer contracts must be displayed with a red warning symbol

questions.explanation: Denning LJ in Spurling v Bradshaw [1956] articulated that some clauses are so wide and unusual that they must be specifically drawn to the other party's attention — 'printed in red ink with a red hand pointing to it'. The more onerous the clause, the more conspicuous the notice required. This principle was developed in Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking where the clause excluded even liability for injury — requiring exceptional notice.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the contra proferentem rule of construction?

options.A: An exclusion clause must be construed in favour of the party seeking to rely on it
options.B: Any ambiguity in an exclusion clause is construed against the party seeking to rely on it (the proferens)
options.C: Exclusion clauses must be read broadly to achieve the parties' commercial intentions
options.D: Exclusion clauses are construed in the same way as other contractual terms

questions.explanation: The contra proferentem rule provides that any ambiguity or doubt in the wording of an exclusion clause is resolved against the party relying upon it (the proferens). Because exclusion clauses restrict rights, any uncertain or equivocal language will be construed narrowly against the drafter. The courts insist that the language used must clearly and unambiguously cover the liability the party seeks to exclude.

questions.question: In Photo Production Ltd v Securicor Transport Ltd [1980] (HL), the House of Lords held that a broadly-worded exclusion clause covered Securicor's employee deliberately setting fire to the claimant's factory. The case confirmed which principle?

options.A: Exclusion clauses can never cover deliberate or fundamental breaches
options.B: Clear and unambiguous language can exclude liability even for fundamental breaches or deliberate wrongdoing — the question is always one of construction
options.C: Securicor was liable in tort but not contract
options.D: The contra proferentem rule required construction against Securicor

questions.explanation: In Photo Production v Securicor [1980], the House of Lords rejected the doctrine of 'fundamental breach' as a rule of law. Lord Diplock held that the question is always one of construction — whether the exclusion clause, on its proper construction, covers the loss that occurred. If the words are sufficiently clear, they can exclude liability even for fundamental breach or deliberate wrongdoing by a party's employee. UCTA now provides statutory control.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly summarises the position on exclusion of liability for negligence?

options.A: Negligence can never be excluded by an exclusion clause in commercial contracts
options.B: To exclude negligence liability, the clause must either expressly mention 'negligence' or the circumstances must be such that the only liability that could arise is in negligence
options.C: Negligence is automatically covered by any generally worded exclusion clause
options.D: Exclusion of negligence is only possible in business-to-business contracts, not consumer contracts

questions.explanation: To exclude negligence liability, following Canada Steamship Lines v R [1952] (PC), the clause must: (1) expressly refer to negligence using that word or an equivalent; or (2) be wide enough to cover negligence if, in the context, the only liability to which the clause could apply is negligence. If there are other heads of liability besides negligence, the courts will not automatically extend the clause to cover negligence.

questions.question: The Consumer Rights Act 2022 (Ireland) (implementing the EU Consumer Rights Directive) provides that exclusion clauses in consumer contracts that attempt to exclude liability for personal injury or death caused by the trader's negligence are:

options.A: Valid provided they are in clear and plain language
options.B: Voidable at the consumer's option
options.C: Void and of no effect — they cannot exclude liability for personal injury or death caused by negligence
options.D: Valid only if agreed in writing by the consumer

questions.explanation: Under Irish consumer protection legislation (reflecting EU Directives and building on the European Communities (Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts) Regulations 1995, now superseded by the CRA 2022 framework), clauses seeking to exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence are void and of no effect. Consumers cannot be deprived of the right to seek compensation for personal injury or death.

questions.question: A course of dealing can incorporate an exclusion clause even without specific notice at the time of the contract. Which case best illustrates this?

options.A: Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971]
options.B: Hollier v Rambler Motors [1972]
options.C: Spurling (J) v Bradshaw [1956]
options.D: Curtis v Chemical Cleaning Co [1951]

questions.explanation: In Spurling v Bradshaw [1956], the Court of Appeal held that an exclusion clause on a warehouseman's receipt was incorporated because the parties had dealt together on those terms for many years. A consistent and regular course of dealing between parties can incorporate terms that are not specifically highlighted on each occasion, provided the party against whom they are relied upon knew of their existence.

questions.question: In Hollier v Rambler Motors [1972], the Court of Appeal held that a clause excluding liability for fire damage had NOT been incorporated into the contract by a course of dealing because:

options.A: The clause had not been signed by the plaintiff
options.B: Only three or four transactions over five years was insufficiently regular and consistent to amount to a course of dealing incorporating the clause
options.C: The clause was void under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
options.D: The clause was printed too small to constitute reasonable notice

questions.explanation: In Hollier v Rambler Motors [1972], the Court of Appeal held that three or four transactions over five years was not a sufficiently regular or consistent course of dealing to incorporate the exclusion clause. A course of dealing must be regular and consistent — it is a question of fact in each case. The court contrasted this with the continuous business dealings in Spurling v Bradshaw.

questions.question: In Curtis v Chemical Cleaning and Dyeing Co [1951], the Court of Appeal held that a broadly-worded exclusion clause did not protect the defendant because:

options.A: The clause was not in plain language as required by statute
options.B: The defendant's employee had made a misrepresentation as to the scope of the clause, limiting its effect to a narrower category of damage
options.C: The exclusion clause covered negligence but not breach of contract
options.D: The clause had not been incorporated because it was written on the back of the receipt

questions.explanation: In Curtis v Chemical Cleaning [1951], the shop assistant told the customer the exclusion clause only covered sequins and beading. In fact, it purported to exclude all damage. The Court of Appeal held that the misrepresentation by the assistant as to the scope of the clause limited its effect — the defendant could not rely on the clause to exclude liability for damage to the dress itself, as the customer had been misled about what she was agreeing to.

questions.question: Under the traditional common law rule established in Chapelton v Barry UDC [1940], a condition on the back of a deckchair ticket was not incorporated. Why?

options.A: Tickets for recreational activities can never incorporate exclusion clauses
options.B: The claimant had no reasonable notice of the conditions — the ticket was a mere receipt for payment, not a contractual document
options.C: The exclusion clause was too onerous under the red hand rule
options.D: The contract was formed at the time of booking and the ticket was issued afterwards

questions.explanation: In Chapelton v Barry UDC [1940], the Court of Appeal held that the conditions on the back of the deckchair ticket were not incorporated. The ticket was a receipt for payment, not a document that a reasonable person would expect to contain contractual conditions. Contrast this with railway or theatre tickets, which are commonly understood to carry conditions.

questions.question: In the context of exemption clauses and the doctrine of fundamental breach (now historical), Photo Production v Securicor [1980] established that:

options.A: A fundamental breach automatically terminates any exclusion clause
options.B: The doctrine of fundamental breach as a rule of law is incorrect — the enforceability of an exclusion clause after a fundamental breach is always a question of construction
options.C: Fundamental breach is a separate cause of action from breach of condition
options.D: Exclusion clauses covering fundamental breach are void as a matter of public policy

questions.explanation: In Photo Production v Securicor [1980], the House of Lords definitively rejected the doctrine of fundamental breach as a rule of law that automatically struck down exclusion clauses after a fundamental breach. Lord Diplock confirmed that whether an exclusion clause covers the particular breach is always a question of construction of the contract. The courts do not apply a rule of law; they construe the clause and the circumstances.

questions.question: Which of the following situations would most likely lead to an exclusion clause being held ineffective due to the rules of incorporation?

options.A: An exclusion clause printed on the reverse of a signed order form where the front refers to conditions on the reverse
options.B: An exclusion clause communicated verbally after the contract has already been concluded
options.C: An exclusion clause in a standard printed contract used consistently between two commercial parties over three years
options.D: An exclusion clause referred to on a website before the customer places an order

questions.explanation: An exclusion clause communicated after the contract has been concluded cannot be incorporated — it is too late. In Olley v Marlborough Court Hotel [1949], a notice in a hotel room was not incorporated because the contract was made at the reception desk before the guest saw the room notice. Communication must occur before or at the time of contracting, not afterwards.

questions.question: What is the effect of a term in a contract that purports to restrict a consumer's right to reject defective goods under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 (Ireland)?

options.A: The term is valid if it is in plain language and conspicuously displayed
options.B: The term is void and unenforceable — statutory consumer rights under the CRA 2022 cannot be restricted or excluded
options.C: The term is voidable at the option of the consumer
options.D: The term is valid in business-to-business contracts only

questions.explanation: The Consumer Rights Act 2022 confers mandatory rights on consumers that cannot be excluded or restricted by any contractual term. Any term that purports to exclude or limit these rights is void. This reflects the EU Consumer Rights framework and ensures that consumers retain the right to reject goods, seek repair or replacement, and claim a price reduction or refund as appropriate.

questions.question: In the Irish case of Sheehan v Breccia [2018], the Court of Appeal considered the interpretation of contractual exclusion clauses. The judgment reaffirmed which principle?

options.A: Exclusion clauses in commercial contracts are presumed void unless the party relying on them proves they are reasonable
options.B: Exclusion clauses are construed strictly and any ambiguity is resolved against the party seeking to rely on them, particularly in the context of liability for negligence
options.C: Commercial parties cannot exclude liability for breach of contract
options.D: Exclusion clauses in loan agreements are governed exclusively by statute

questions.explanation: In Sheehan v Breccia [2018], the Irish Court of Appeal applied well-established principles of construction to exclusion clauses in a commercial context. The court emphasised that exclusion clauses are construed strictly and contra proferentem — ambiguities are resolved against the party relying on the clause. The court also confirmed that clear and unambiguous language is required to exclude negligence.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes a 'limitation of liability' clause, as distinct from an 'exclusion' clause?

options.A: A limitation clause excludes all liability; an exclusion clause limits liability to a fixed financial cap
options.B: A limitation clause caps financial liability at a specified amount or type of loss; an exclusion clause seeks to exclude all liability entirely
options.C: Limitation clauses are valid in all contracts; exclusion clauses are always void
options.D: There is no legal distinction between limitation and exclusion clauses

questions.explanation: An exclusion clause seeks to exclude all liability for particular types of breach or loss entirely. A limitation clause restricts the extent of liability — for example, capping damages at a specified monetary sum, or limiting liability to direct losses only. Both types of clause are subject to the same rules of incorporation and construction, and limitation clauses are generally viewed more favourably by courts because they acknowledge that some liability exists.

questions.question: Which Irish Act gave courts the power to strike down unreasonable exclusion clauses in consumer contracts before the CRA 2022?

options.A: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (enacted in Ireland)
options.B: The European Communities (Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts) Regulations 1995 (SI 27 of 1995)
options.C: The Consumer Protection Act 2007
options.D: The Sale of Goods Act 1893, s55

questions.explanation: Before the CRA 2022, the primary protection against unfair terms in consumer contracts in Ireland was the European Communities (Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts) Regulations 1995 (SI 27 of 1995), implementing the EU Unfair Terms Directive (93/13/EEC). These Regulations provided that unfair terms in consumer contracts were not binding on consumers. Ireland did not enact a direct equivalent of the UK's UCTA 1977.

questions.question: For an exclusion clause to be effective at common law, it must satisfy two requirements. What are they?

options.A: It must be signed and witnessed, and it must be stated in capital letters
options.B: It must be incorporated into the contract and, on its proper construction, must cover the loss or breach in question
options.C: It must be reasonable and not contrary to public policy
options.D: It must be disclosed to the other party's solicitor and approved by a court

questions.explanation: At common law, the two requirements for an effective exclusion clause are: (1) incorporation — the clause must be part of the contract (by signature, notice, or course of dealing); and (2) construction — on its true construction, the clause must cover the particular breach or loss that has occurred. If either requirement fails, the clause will not afford protection. Statutory controls (CRA 2022, SGSSA 1980) then apply additional layers of protection.
